items.headline: City updates affordable housing protections for expiring subsidy contracts
items.summary: San José City Council addressed affordability restrictions for housing projects when federal subsidies expire or buildings face foreclosure. This affects existing affordable housing stock and determines whether units remain affordable long-term as contracts wind down.

items.headline: Storm sewer system gets city-wide master planning update
items.summary: The city expanded its consultant agreement for comprehensive storm sewer infrastructure planning across San José. This planning effort could inform future flood prevention, drainage improvements, and stormwater management investments.

items.headline: Comm Hill Phase 3.1 housing development moves forward
items.summary: Four new homes permitted on Altino Boulevard as part of the Comm Hill Phase 3.1 project, ranging from $487K-$573K. This represents a significant cluster of residential development in a single phase of a larger master-planned community.

items.headline: Sherly Place adds three new homes to neighborhood
items.summary: Three single-family homes permitted at Sherly Place (lots 2-4), valued between $496K-$570K. Completes the initial development phase for this residential community.

items.headline: South Bay water recycling pump station project cost increases
items.summary: The South Bay Water Recycling Pump Station 5 Bypass Project required a contingency increase after rebidding. This regional wastewater facility project affects water recycling capacity for South Bay communities.

items.headline: City Council reviews FY 2026-27 Capital Improvement Program
items.summary: The council held a study session to discuss Mountain View's capital projects and infrastructure spending for the next fiscal year. This is where major public investments in roads, facilities, and development projects get debated before final approval.

items.headline: Sunnyvale considers changes to tenant protection rules
items.summary: The City Council introduced an ordinance to amend the Residential Tenant Protections Program, signaling potential changes to renter safeguards in the city. Details on what protections would be added or modified were not specified in the agenda.
